title: Huevos a la flamenca
subtitle: Andalusian baked eggs with chorizo and tomato sauce
excerpt: Eggs baked directly in a thick tomato and pepper sauce with slices of chorizo and a scatter of peas, finished under the grill until the whites are just set and the yolks are still liquid. An Andalusian tapa that eats like a full meal.
sourceNotes: Huevos a la flamenca is a classic dish from Seville and the wider Andalusia region. It appears in early twentieth-century Spanish cookbooks and is associated with the taverna culture of Seville. The combination of eggs baked in tomato sauce with cured sausage is ancient in Spanish cooking; the flamenca designation refers to the region rather than any connection to flamenco dancing.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The tomato sauce base can be made up to 2 days ahead and refrigerated. Warm, then add eggs and bake to order.

content.text: The sauce is the dish. It needs to be thick enough that the eggs sit in wells rather than floating — if it is too thin, the whites will spread and cook unevenly. Cook the pepper and onion down properly before the tomatoes go in: they need 10 minutes of patient cooking to become soft and sweet, which is what gives the sauce its depth.

The eggs go in raw and cook entirely from the heat of the surrounding sauce plus 8 to 10 minutes in a moderate oven. The aim is set whites and barely-liquid yolks — remove from the oven as soon as the whites are opaque. Carry-over heat will set them a little further.

Heat the {{olive-oil}} in an ovenproof frying pan or casserole over medium heat. Add the {{chorizo}} slices and cook for 2 minutes per side until lightly crisped. Remove with a slotted spoon and set aside. In the same pan, add the {{onion}} and {{pepper-red}} and cook over medium heat for 10 minutes, stirring occasionally, until very soft. Add the {{garlic}} and {{paprika-smoked}} and cook for 1 minute. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} and season well. Simmer for 10 minutes until the sauce has thickened considerably. Stir in the {{peas-frozen}}. Return the chorizo to the pan.

Preheat the oven to 180°C fan. Make 4 wells in the sauce with the back of a spoon. Crack an {{eggs}} egg into each well. Transfer to the oven and bake for 8 to 10 minutes until the whites are just set and the yolks remain liquid. Scatter {{parsley-flat}} over and serve directly from the pan with crusty bread.

items.symptom: Whites are set but yolks are also set and hard
items.cause: Cooked too long or oven too hot
items.fix: Check at 8 minutes — the eggs continue to cook from residual heat after the pan leaves the oven; err on the side of undercooking

items.symptom: Eggs are raw in the centre even after 12 minutes
items.cause: Sauce was too cold when the eggs went in, or oven temperature too low
items.fix: Bring the sauce to a simmer on the hob before adding the eggs; use an oven thermometer to verify temperature

items.symptom: Sauce is watery and eggs sink to the bottom
items.cause: Sauce not reduced enough before adding eggs
items.fix: Simmer the sauce for longer before the eggs go in — it should be thick enough to hold a well

items.cause: Not rendered properly before the sauce was built
items.fix: Cook the chorizo slices properly until the fat runs before removing; drain on kitchen paper if needed

content.text: Huevos a la flamenca is the definitive dish of the Sevillian bodega — the kind of place with barrels along the walls and plates that arrive quickly and without ceremony. It belongs to the tradition of huevos a la española, eggs cooked in tomato-based sauces that run through Andalusian and Castilian cooking. The dish crosses freely between tapa and main course depending on portion size and what comes before it. In Seville it is eaten at any hour, which is itself a fair summary of Andalusian cooking culture.
